nativeRange.habitat: Shallow flooded grasslands and spring-fed wetlands at approximately 1,040 m elevation in the Shan State highlands; clear, well-oxygenated water with dense aquatic vegetation, fine sediment, and leaf litter; associated with the Salween River drainage

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java moss, fine-leaved stem plants, floating plants)
driftwood roots and branches for shade and structure

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on small insects, worms, tiny crustaceans, and other zooplankton. In captivity, eagerly accepts live and frozen micro-foods: baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), daphnia, cyclops, microworms, and mini bloodworm. Also takes small sinking pellets, crushed flake, and powdered fry foods. Biofilm grazing supplements commercial foods. Feed 2–3 small meals daily; fast metabolism and small size mean this species benefits from frequent feeding. Newly imported specimens are often semi-emaciated and should be offered live foods initially.

avoidWith: fish larger than 5 cm (risk of competition and predation on rosy loaches)
fast-moving boisterous species that outcompete at feeding

summary: The Rosy Loach is a tiny undescribed nemacheilid from highland Myanmar, reaching only 2–3 cm. Males flush vivid orange-pink during courtship; females are brownish with dark spots. A prolific shoaler needing groups of 8+, it thrives in cool-tolerant, well-planted nano setups and is one of the most active and rewarding micro-loaches in the hobby.
careNotes:
The Rosy Loach (Petruichthys sp. ‘rosy’) is an undescribed nemacheilid first collected from spring-fed wetlands in the Shan State highlands of eastern Myanmar, at the same type locality as the Celestial Pearl Danio. It remains scientifically undescribed; older trade names include Tuberoschistura sp. ‘rosy’ and Yunnanilus sp. ‘rosy’. At 2–3 cm, it is one of the smallest loaches available in the hobby and qualifies as a true nano fish.

A biologically mature, well-established tank is non-negotiable: this species is highly susceptible to ammonia and nitrite spikes that larger fish may tolerate, and should never be added to a tank less than 2 months old. Stable water chemistry is the primary care challenge and the reason this species is rated intermediate rather than beginner. A sponge filter or pre-filtered HOB is recommended to prevent fry or juveniles from being sucked in.

Keep in groups of at least 8–10 individuals; smaller groups produce chronic stress and muted coloration. Unlike most loaches, this species spends considerable time in open midwater and forms active schools. Males display bright orange to rosy-red coloration that intensifies during courtship; females are notably larger, brownish, and spotted.

Fine sand substrate (pool filter sand, play sand, or dedicated aquarium sand) is strongly preferred to protect the sensory barbels used in foraging. Dense planting with floating cover and Java moss or other fine-leaved mosses replicates the shallow vegetated grassland biotope and reduces stress. Water temperature of 20–26°C suits the cool highland origin; the species tolerates the lower end of this range well, making it compatible with cool-water nano tanks.

Due to small size, take care with tankmate selection: avoid any fish above 5 cm, aggressive species, or fast boisterous feeders that prevent the loaches from accessing food. Ideal companions are other Myanmar highland species such as celestial pearl danios, or similarly sized peaceful nano fish. Neocaridina shrimp adults are generally safe, though fry may be predated.

Post-import specimens frequently arrive semi-emaciated; offer live Artemia nauplii, daphnia, or microworms multiple times daily during the first weeks to restore condition. Long-term, feed 2–3 small meals per day given the high metabolism.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is achievable and increasingly common. The Rosy Loach is an egg-scatterer with no parental care; eggs are deposited in small batches of 1–3 among fine-leaved plants or moss. A densely planted species tank or a dedicated breeding setup with a thick Java moss carpet is recommended. Raising water temperature toward the upper end of the range (25–26°C) and increasing live-food feeding frequency triggers spawning behaviour. Males actively chase females; spawning is often spontaneous in well-established planted tanks. Eggs incubate in approximately 24 hours at 26–28°C; yolk sac is absorbed within 24–36 hours post-hatch. Fry are very small and require infusoria or 5–50 micron powdered fry foods initially, progressing to Artemia nauplii and microworms as they grow. Adults readily consume eggs and fry, so remove adults or use a separate breeding container. Fine-leaved moss (Taxiphyllum sp.) offers some egg protection if raising fry alongside adults.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Petruichthys sp. ‘rosy’ (Rosy Loach)
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy (Nemacheilidae, Cypriniformes), adult size 25–30 mm SL, temperature 20–26°C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness 90–268 ppm TDS, minimum tank footprint 60×30 cm, native range (Shan State Myanmar, ~1040 m, Salween drainage), habitat description, diet (micropredator), behaviour (gregarious, 8–10+ specimens), sexual dimorphism, breeding (egg-scatterer, batches of 1–3, 24-hour incubation), fry foods, and import care notes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Top 10 Loaches (Rosy Loach entry)
additional.notes: Trade-focused care notes. Confirmed adult size 1–1.25 inches, group keeping in 5-gallon+, importance of mature biofilm, diet of frozen cyclops and Easy Fry food, successful captive breeding method (heavy planting, remove adults after spawning). No dedicated rosy loach page exists; data extracted from loach top-10 article.

additional.name: AquariumStoreDepot — Rosy Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-referenced water parameters: temperature 20–26°C, pH 6.5–7.5, GH 5–12, KH 3–6. Minimum tank size 38 L (10 gal). Adult size 2.5–3 cm. Lifespan 5–7 years. Fine sand substrate requirement and 2–3× daily feeding schedule confirmed. Compatible species list.

additional.name: Aquadiction — Rosy Loach Species Spotlight
additional.notes: Cross-referenced temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.5–8.0, GH 8–20, TDS 90–268 ppm. Maximum length 3 cm. Groups of 8+. Shan State, eastern Myanmar. Confirmed Nemacheilidae, Cypriniformes. Lifespan up to 8 years noted (used 5–7 per majority of sources).

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Rosy Loach (Tuberoschistura arakanensis) Care
additional.notes: Cross-referenced adult size 1–1.25 inches, lifespan 5–7 years, pH 6.5–8.0, temperature 74–82°F. Myanmar and Thailand range noted. Egg-scattering spawner confirmed. Minimum 20-gallon for groups of 8–10 noted (conservative; 40 L used per SeriouslyFish footprint guidance for nano group).

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; spawns in fine-leaved plants or moss in batches of 1–3 eggs. No parental care — adults eat eggs and fry. Dense Java moss or spawning mop required; adults should be removed post-spawning. Eggs hatch ~24 hours; fry free-swim within 24–36 hours post-hatch. Fry require infusoria or powdered micro-fry foods initially. Captive breeding achievable in well-established, heavily planted tanks with live-food conditioning.
